    3C 84 / NGC 1275

    3C 84 / NGC 1275

    Type: Radio galaxy / AGN
    Level: Specialist / Research
    Other names: Perseus A, 3C 84, B0316+413
    Host galaxy: NGC 1275 (cD galaxy, Perseus cluster BCG)
    MBH: ~1.1×109 M⊙
    Jet viewing angle: ~45°
    Related: Faraday rotation
    Bondi accretion
    RIAF/ADAF models
    Recollimation shocks

    3C 84 is the powerful radio source associated with NGC 1275 (Perseus A), the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) at the center of the Perseus cluster. It is one of the brightest radio sources in the sky and hosts a sub-relativistic jet that has been monitored for decades with VLBI [1][2]. The jet viewing angle is approximately 45° [3][4]. The current episode of jet activity began around 2003 [5][6].

    Parsec-Scale Jet Structure

    VLBI monitoring reveals several distinct jet components [1][7]:

    • Core: The 43 GHz VLBI core lies within ~0.03–0.08 mas of the black hole [8][9]. Core polarization remains undetected [1].
    • C4 / C4a: Inner jet components at ~3 mas from core. First detected in polarization in 2022 December with RM = 2.6–3.5×105 rad/m2; C4a showed RM = (6.1±1.1)×105 rad/m2 [1][7].
    • C3: A hotspot region at ~4–5 mas. The bright eastern limb emerged in late 2015 [2][10] and was disrupted after 2017 May [11]. Average RM = (3.9±0.6)×105 rad/m2 from VLBA [1]; KVN values of 1.0–3.6×105 rad/m2 (2018–2024) [1].
    • K: Downstream at 5–7 mas, with the smallest RM values (0.5–0.9×105 rad/m2) [1].
    • Southern lobe: At ~15 mas (~7.5 pc). RM = 7000 rad/m2, confirmed stable over 20 years [12][1].

    The jet shows evidence of instabilities and precession [13][14]. Gamma-ray flares in 2022 November–2023 January coincided with polarization emergence at C4 [15][16].

    Faraday Rotation Profile

    The defining observational result is that all RM measurements from 1–15 pc follow a single power-law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 [1]. This unifies decades of seemingly discrepant measurements:

    • IRAM PdBI non-detections at 81–348 GHz with RM dispersion ≥ 106 rad/m2 [17][18]
    • CARMA/SMA 1.3 mm detection with RM = 8.7×105 rad/m2 [19]
    • VLBA 43 GHz C3 RM = (3–6)×105 rad/m2 [2]
    • VLBA 8–22 GHz southern lobe RM = 7000 rad/m2 [12]

    The Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium within the Bondi radius (11.8 pc), not a RIAF, jet boundary layer, or individual gas cloud [1].

    magnetic field

    The RM profile constrains B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius, with B = 0.1–1.5 μG at 11.8 pc increasing rapidly inward [1]. This is the first spatially resolved ambient magnetic field measurement in an elliptical galaxy [1]. Previous estimates were galaxy-averaged [20] or theoretical [21][22].

    Perseus Cluster Environment

    NGC 1275 hosts extensive optical emission-line filaments extending >100 kpc, dragged outward by radio bubbles and carrying magnetic fields of ~24 μG [23][24]. X-ray observations show electron density ne ∝ r−0.67 on kpc scales [25]. The interplay between AGN feedback, cold gas precipitation, and the hot ICM creates a complex multiphase environment [26][27].

    Accretion State

    The Eddington ratio of 3C 84 is revised from the commonly quoted 0.4% [19] down to ~0.04–0.09% [1], placing it at the boundary of the accretion state transition where BLR emission weakens and RIAF models apply [28][29]. The coexistence of broad emission lines, an FFA disk, and a parsec-scale ambient medium places interesting constraints on accretion models [1].

    See Also

    • Park et al. (2026) — Unified RM profile paper
    • Faraday Rotation — Underlying physics
    • Bondi Accretion — Accretion framework
    • RIAF/ADAF Models — Competing Faraday screen model

    References
